front: Stationary sequence (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A sequence X_0, X_1, ... is stationary when each shifted finite block has the same law as the original block. Exam use: Use as the entry point for ergodic averages, stationary Markov chains, rotations, and stationary increments. Pitfall: Stationarity is distributional, not pathwise equality; it does not imply independence.

front: Measure-preserving transformation model (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Every stationary sequence on a nice state space can be represented as X_n(omega)=X(phi^n omega) for a measure-preserving map phi. Exam use: Lets all ergodic-theorem statements be proved for one abstract dynamical system. Pitfall: The representation changes the sample space; it preserves laws but not necessarily the user's original probability space.

front: Invariant events and invariant sigma-field (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: An event A is invariant if phi^{-1}A=A up to null sets; the invariant events form the sigma-field I. Exam use: The limit in Birkhoff's theorem is the conditional expectation onto I. Pitfall: Invariant means unchanged under the transformation, not merely high probability overlap after one shift.

front: Ergodicity (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A measure-preserving transformation is ergodic when every invariant event has probability 0 or 1. Exam use: Use ergodicity to turn Birkhoff limits E(X|I) into constants EX. Pitfall: Ergodic is stronger than stationary and different from independent; periodic examples can be stationary but not mixing.

front: IID shifts are ergodic (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: For an iid sequence, invariant shift events lie in the tail sigma-field, so Kolmogorov's 0-1 law implies ergodicity. Exam use: Explains why Birkhoff's theorem recovers the strong law for iid sequences. Pitfall: The tail sigma-field argument uses iid independence; it is not automatic for every stationary sequence.

front: Stationary Markov chain ergodicity criterion (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A countable-state Markov chain started in a positive stationary distribution is ergodic exactly when the chain is irreducible. Exam use: Use for time averages of irreducible positive recurrent chains. Pitfall: Irreducibility makes I trivial, but periodic chains may still have a nontrivial tail sigma-field.

front: Circle rotation ergodicity (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Rotation x -> x+theta mod 1 is ergodic under Lebesgue measure when theta is irrational and not ergodic when theta is rational. Exam use: The canonical deterministic example where ergodic averages behave like probabilistic averages. Pitfall: Rational rotations have finite orbits and many invariant unions of orbit slices.

front: Functions of stationary processes (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Measurable functions of the future of a stationary process form another stationary process; ergodicity is inherited under such factors. Exam use: Useful for creating stationary indicator, block, or return-time processes. Pitfall: The function must be applied in a shift-compatible way; arbitrary time-dependent functions can destroy stationarity.

front: Birkhoff ergodic theorem (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For X in L1, the time averages n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X(phi^m omega) converge almost surely and in L1 to E(X|I). Exam use: Main tool for replacing long-run empirical averages by conditional or ordinary means. Pitfall: Without ergodicity the limit is random: it is E(X|I), not necessarily EX.

front: Maximal ergodic lemma (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For partial sums S_k of X along a measure-preserving orbit and M_k=max(0,S_1,...,S_k), one has E(X; M_k>0) >= 0. Exam use: The key technical inequality behind Birkhoff's pointwise convergence proof. Pitfall: It is an integration inequality, not a direct maximal probability bound; track the set {M_k>0}.

front: Strong law as an ergodic theorem (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For iid X_m in L1, Birkhoff gives n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X_m -> E X_0 almost surely and in L1. Exam use: Use as a conceptual shortcut from Chapter 2 LLNs to general stationary sequences. Pitfall: The theorem needs integrability; for nonintegrable iid variables this conclusion can fail.

front: Stationary Markov chain time averages (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For an irreducible countable-state Markov chain started from its stationary distribution pi, n^{-1} sum_{m<n} f(X_m) -> sum_x f(x) pi(x) for pi-integrable f. Exam use: Recovers the long-run frequency and reward law for positive recurrent Markov chains. Pitfall: The statement is under stationarity; nonstationary initial laws need an additional argument.

front: Weyl equidistribution from irrational rotation (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For irrational theta, the orbit x+n theta mod 1 visits every interval [a,b) with limiting frequency b-a for every starting x. Exam use: Use for deterministic averaging and number-theoretic distribution mod 1. Pitfall: Birkhoff first gives almost-every starting point; the interval theorem needs extra work for every x.

front: Benford law for powers (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: If log_10 2 is irrational, the first digit of 2^m has limiting frequency log_10((k+1)/k) for digit k. Exam use: A memorable application of equidistribution to leading-digit asymptotics. Pitfall: Benford frequencies describe a limiting distribution, not exact finite-sample frequencies.

front: Lp convergence upgrade (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: If X is in Lp for p>1, the Birkhoff averages converge in Lp as well as almost surely. Exam use: Useful when a problem asks for convergence in norm rather than only almost surely. Pitfall: Do not infer Lp convergence from L1 convergence without an Lp bound or uniform integrability.

front: Range growth for stationary increments (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary-increment walk S_n, the range R_n=|{S_1,...,S_n}| satisfies R_n/n -> E(1_A|I), where A is the event of never returning to 0 after time 0. Exam use: Connects recurrence questions to an ergodic average of escape events. Pitfall: The limit is conditional on I; only in ergodic cases does it reduce to P(A).

front: Zero conditional drift implies recurrence on Z (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For stationary integer-valued increments with E|X_1|<infinity and E(X_1|I)=0, the walk returns to 0 infinitely often almost surely. Exam use: Generalizes the Chung-Fuchs recurrence intuition beyond iid increments. Pitfall: The condition is E(X_1|I)=0, not merely EX_1=0; mixtures of positive and negative drifts are counterexamples.

front: Kac return-time theorem (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary process that hits A almost surely, the successive return gaps to A are stationary under P(.|X_0 in A), and E(T_1|X_0 in A)=1/P(X_0 in A). Exam use: Turns visits to a set into regenerative cycles and recovers mean return time 1/pi(x) for Markov chains. Pitfall: Conditioning on X_0 in A is essential; the first waiting time from an arbitrary stationary time has a length-biased flavor.

front: Cycle trick for stationary measures (6.3 Recurrence)
back: Expected occupation of B during a return cycle to A equals P(X_0 in B)/P(X_0 in A) when A and B are disjoint and A is recurrent. Exam use: Useful for constructing stationary measures of Markov chains from excursions. Pitfall: The denominator is the stationary mass of A; the formula is not a conditional probability of hitting B before returning.

front: Stationary renewal waiting-time bias (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary {0,1} process conditioned on X_0=1, the first return law satisfies Pbar(T_1=n)=Pbar(T_1>=n)/Ebar T_1 in the iid-gap renewal case. Exam use: Explains inspection-paradox corrections in stationary renewal processes. Pitfall: The distribution seen from a stationary time differs from the raw interarrival distribution.

front: Subadditive ergodic theorem (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: For a stationary subadditive array X_{m,n} with integrability and a linear lower expectation bound, X_{0,n}/n converges almost surely and in L1 to a limit with mean inf_m E X_{0,m}/m. Exam use: The main tool for limits of optimal costs, passage times, longest subsequences, and products. Pitfall: Check the exact stationarity assumptions; Liggett's version is weaker than Kingman's original in useful applications.

front: Fekete step for expected subadditive arrays (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: If a_n=E X_{0,n} and a_m+a_{n-m}>=a_n, then a_n/n converges to inf_m a_m/m. Exam use: Use whenever the deterministic expectation part of a subadditive theorem appears. Pitfall: The inequality direction is easy to mix up because the array convention is X_{0,m}+X_{m,n}>=X_{0,n}.

front: Birkhoff as the additive special case (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: If X_{m,n}=xi_{m+1}+...+xi_n for a stationary integrable sequence, then the subadditive theorem reduces to Birkhoff. Exam use: A quick consistency check for the hypotheses of the subadditive theorem. Pitfall: Do not lose the indexing: X_{m,n} covers increments after m through n.

front: Range as a subadditive example (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: The number of distinct sites visited between times m+1 and n is subadditive, so the normalized range has an almost sure and L1 limit. Exam use: Provides a second route to existence of range-growth rates even when the rate is not identified. Pitfall: The theorem gives existence of the limit, not the recurrence classification by itself.

front: Longest common subsequence limit (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: For two ergodic stationary sequences, the longest common subsequence length L_{0,n} satisfies L_{0,n}/n -> gamma, where gamma=sup_m E L_{0,m}/m. Exam use: Recognize optimization lengths as candidates for subadditive limits. Pitfall: The theorem proves existence of gamma but usually not its numerical value.

front: Products of positive random matrices (6.5 Applications)
back: For stationary positive matrices with integrable log entries, n^{-1} log of product entries converges almost surely to a common growth rate. Exam use: A probability route to Lyapunov-exponent-type limits. Pitfall: Strict positivity and log-integrability matter; zeros or heavy tails can break the logarithmic comparison.

front: Increasing subsequences in random permutations (6.5 Applications)
back: Poissonizing random permutations and applying subadditivity shows the longest increasing subsequence has an n^{1/2} order limit constant. Exam use: A classic example of turning a combinatorial optimization problem into an ergodic limit. Pitfall: The subadditive argument gives the existence of the constant; identifying it as 2 requires deeper work.

front: Poisson square time change (6.5 Applications)
back: If tau(n) is the side length of the square containing the first n points of a rate-one planar Poisson process, then tau(n)/sqrt(n) -> 1 almost surely. Exam use: Used to pass from Poissonized increasing paths back to fixed-size random permutations. Pitfall: The scale is sqrt(n) because the square area is t^2.

front: First-passage percolation time constant (6.5 Applications)
back: For iid nonnegative edge passage times on Z^d with suitable integrability, t(0,nu)/n converges almost surely to a deterministic time constant. Exam use: Core template for metric growth models and random media. Pitfall: Finite mean of one edge is sufficient in the simple statement but can be weakened; without integrability the limit may be infinite.

front: First-passage percolation integrability condition (6.5 Applications)
back: A weaker useful condition is that the minimum of 2d independent edge times has finite mean, equivalently integral_0^infty (1-F(x))^{2d} dx < infinity. Exam use: Helps check when a finite time constant exists for heavy-tailed edge weights. Pitfall: This condition concerns the minimum of several edges, not the edge variable itself.

front: Age-dependent branching process speed (6.5 Applications)
back: In an age-dependent branching process with no extinction at birth and finite lifetimes, the first birth time in generation n divided by n converges almost surely. Exam use: Shows why the weaker Liggett hypotheses are useful beyond Kingman's original stationary-array assumptions. Pitfall: The stronger all-triples subadditivity can fail because the fastest later descendant need not descend from the fastest earlier individual.

front: Branching speed via lifetime large deviations (6.5 Applications)
back: For mean offspring mu and lifetime sums with rate c(a), the asymptotic earliest-generation speed is gamma=inf{a: log mu - c(a)>0}. Exam use: Connects subadditive limits with large-deviation rate functions. Pitfall: The subadditive theorem gives existence; this formula requires additional branching and large-deviation analysis.

front: Invariant sigma-field and invariant random variables (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Show invariant events form a sigma-field by pullback closure; prove an I-measurable random variable is invariant by checking rational cuts, and conversely use invariant inverse images. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.1; identifying E(X|I) and invariant limits. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Strict representative for almost invariant sets (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: From an almost invariant set, form a forward pullback union B and then C=intersection phi^{-n}B; C is strictly invariant and differs only by a null set. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.2; replacing mod-null invariance by exact invariance. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Direct density proof of irrational rotation ergodicity (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Use pigeonhole gaps to prove the irrational orbit is dense, approximate positive-measure sets by high-density intervals, and contradict coexistence of invariant A and A^c with positive measure. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.3; proving ergodicity without Fourier series. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Two-sided extension of a stationary sequence (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Define finite-dimensional laws on integer-indexed coordinates by shifting all indices into the nonnegative side, then use stationarity for consistency and Kolmogorov extension. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.4; return-time and cycle arguments needing negative times. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Stationarity and ergodicity pass to factors (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: For Y_k=g(X_k,X_{k+1},...), stationarity follows from shifted future laws; invariant events for Y pull back to invariant events for X, so ergodicity passes to Y. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.5; creating stationary derived processes. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Random phase makes iid blocks stationary and ergodic (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Concatenate iid blocks and start at an independent uniform phase; one-step shifts rotate the phase and occasionally shift the iid block sequence, giving stationarity and ergodicity. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.6; block constructions with stationary output. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Gauss map invariant density by branch telescoping (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: For phi(x)=1/x-floor(1/x), split into branches x=1/(k+y); the transformed density sum telescopes to 1/(1+y). Exam use: Exercise 6.1.7; verifying the continued-fraction invariant measure. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Lp upgrade of Birkhoff by truncation (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Prove Lp convergence for bounded observables by bounded convergence, then truncate X and control the tail using Jensen plus Lp contraction of conditional expectation. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.1; upgrading ergodic averages from L1 to Lp. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Moving observable ergodic average (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Decompose averages of g_m(phi^m) into the fixed g average plus errors; control a.s. errors by tail suprema and L1 errors by Cesaro means. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.2; triangular or time-varying observables in ergodic averages. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Wiener maximal inequality from maximal ergodic lemma (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Apply the maximal ergodic lemma to Y=X-alpha so the positivity event of the shifted partial sums is exactly {max S_j/j > alpha}. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.3; deriving maximal probability bounds for ergodic averages. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Range expectation by last-visit decomposition (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Count each visited site by its last visit time; stationarity makes the probability of no future revisit over n-m steps equal to g_{n-m}. Exam use: Exercise 6.3.1; expected range identities for stationary-increment walks. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Positive drift escape probability from range growth (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: With ergodic integer increments bounded above by one and positive mean, the range grows at the same rate as the running maximum; Theorem 6.3.1 identifies the rate with P(escape). Exam use: Exercise 6.3.2; recurrence/escape probabilities under stationary increments. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Cycle trick occupation ratio (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Use a two-sided stationary version and partition by the last visit to A before time 0; expected B-occupation in an A-cycle equals P(X0 in B)/P(X0 in A). Exam use: Exercise 6.3.3; constructing stationary measures from excursions. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Stationary renewal first-waiting bias (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Under conditioning on a renewal at time 0, stationarity of return gaps gives P(T=n)=Pbar(T>=n)/Ebar T for the waiting time seen from a stationary time. Exam use: Exercise 6.3.4; inspection-paradox and stationary renewal calculations. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Arbitrarily slow deterministic subadditive convergence (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Set X_{m,m+k}=f(k) with f(k)/k decreasing; subadditivity follows by comparing both pieces to f(n)/n, so the expectation convergence can be as slow as f(n)/n. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.1; understanding limits of Fekete-type convergence rates. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Longest common subsequence first-moment bounds (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Compute small-n expectations for lower bounds and bound P(L_n >= K) by the expected number of matching index-pair subsequences, binom(n,K)^2 2^{-K}. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.2; bounding LCS constants. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Poisson greedy path lower bound (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Choose successive Poisson points minimizing x+y in the northeast quadrant; the increment sum has tail exp(-t^2/2), giving mean coordinate step sqrt(pi/8) and gamma >= sqrt(8/pi). Exam use: Exercise 6.5.3; lower bounds for Hammersley's increasing path constant. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Permutation LIS upper bound by first moment (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: For a fixed k-subset of indices, the values are increasing with probability 1/k!, so E J_k^n=binom(n,k)/k!; Stirling with k~alpha sqrt(n) gives gamma <= e. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.4; upper bounds for longest increasing subsequences. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Branching Laplace martingale speed bound (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Normalize the generation-n Laplace sum by (mu phi(theta))^n to get a nonnegative martingale; Markov's inequality bounds early birth events when exp(-theta a)/(mu phi(theta))>1. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.5; age-dependent branching speed upper-tail bounds. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.
